---
title: <span style="color:#548893;">**Advanced Quantitative Methods Clinic (Dissertation-focused)**</span>
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: false
    preview-links: auto
html-math-method:
  method: mathjax
  url: "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-mml-chtml.js"
author:
  - name: "Matias Montero"
  - name: "Henry Tsz Fung Lo"
date: "4 March 2026"
footer: "Advanced Quantitative Methods (Dissertation clinic)"
logo: "images/logo.png"
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

## [**Why this session?**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   This is a dissertation-focused "clinic" for students planning to use
    quantitative methods.
-   We will *revisit and deepen* key topics from the Intro-Intermediate
    sequence.
-   We will then workshop dissertation-specific questions in small
    groups.
:::

::: incremental
-   Goal: help you make defensible design and analysis choices, and help
    you diagnose issues you are already encountering.
:::

## [**What this session is (and is not)**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   **Is:** targeted recap + Q&A + applied troubleshooting for your
    dissertation.
-   **Is:** a space to pressure-test assumptions and analysis plans.
-   **Is not:** teaching an entirely new advanced method from scratch.
:::

::: incremental
-   If you are already exploring a more advanced method, we can discuss
    it in small groups to the extent that you have begun to work with
    it.
:::

## [**Plan for today**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
1.  Short recap of high-leverage topics (decision points for
    dissertation work)
2.  Open Q&A on those topics
3.  Breakouts: dissertation-specific workshops (small groups)
4.  Wrap-up: common patterns, next steps, resources
:::

## [**Recap: checkpoints that matter**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
We will focus on a small number of "checkpoints" that repeatedly drive
dissertation outcomes:

-   Research question

-   Identification

-   Model choice aligned with outcome type and data structure

-   Handling clustering and dependence

-   Missing data and sensitivity

-   Diagnostics, robustness, and transparent reporting
:::

## [**Checkpoint 1: Your research question**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   What is your target quantity? (difference in means, marginal effect,
    causal effect, association, prediction)
-   What is your unit of analysis?
-   What is the counterfactual or comparison you rely on (if causal)?
-   What assumptions would need to be true for your interpretation?
:::

## [**Checkpoint 2: Identification and threats**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   Confounding and selection: what variables must be accounted for (and
    what should *not* be controlled)?
-   Reverse causality and simultaneity
-   Measurement error and construct validity
:::

## [**Checkpoint 3: Model choice and data structure**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   Outcome type: continuous, binary, ordinal, count, time-to-event
-   Dependence: clustering, repeated measures, networks
-   Functional form: non-linearity, interactions
-   Interpretation: coefficients vs marginal effects vs predicted values
:::

## [**Checkpoint 4: Missing data**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   What is missing, for whom, and why?
-   Plausible mechanism: MCAR, MAR, MNAR (what would make each
    plausible?)
-   Strategy: complete-case, multiple imputation, model-based approaches
-   Minimum: report missingness patterns and do a sensitivity check
:::

## [**Checkpoint 5: Diagnostics and robustness**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   Model fit and residual diagnostics (where applicable)
-   Influence/leverage and outliers
-   Alternative specifications that matter (not "specification fishing")
-   Pre-analysis logic: what would change your conclusion?
:::

## [**Checkpoint 6: Reporting and write-up**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   Clear link: question -\> design -\> assumptions -\> estimation -\>
    interpretation
-   Tables/figures that answer the question (not just what the software
    outputs)
-   What to put in main text vs appendix
-   Reproducibility (open science framework): code, data, and
    transparent decisions
:::

## [**Q&A: how we will run it**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   We will start with questions on the recap checkpoints.
-   If a question is highly dissertation-specific, we may park it for
    breakouts.
:::

## [**Breakouts: dissertation workshop format**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   Small groups organised by method/data structure (where possible)
-   Each student brings one concrete problem
-   We aim for "actionable next steps", not perfection
:::

::: incremental
Suggested roles (rotate):

-   Presenter (2 to 3 min)

-   Discussant(s) (group)

-   Scribe (captures decisions and next steps)
:::

## [**What to bring to the breakout**]{style="color:#548893;"}

Bring a short "problem brief" (bullet form is fine):

::: incremental
-   Research question (one sentence)
-   Outcome and key predictors
-   Data structure (N, clusters, waves, source)
-   Planned method/model (what you think you will do)
-   What is the specific decision/block you want help with?
:::

## [**What we can realistically do in breakouts**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   Clarify what the method assumes and whether your design plausibly
    meets it
-   Help you reframe your question into something your data can answer
-   Troubleshoot implementation issues if you have already started
    working with the method
:::

## [**Common problems we expect (examples)**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   "My outcome is ordinal, but everyone around me uses linear models"
-   "I have clustering and small numbers of clusters"
-   "Missingness seems related to my outcome"
:::

## [**Let's start the breakout!**]{style="color:#548893;"}

## [**Thank you**]{style="color:#548893;"}

::: incremental
-   If you did not get to raise your question, follow up by email.
-   This is our first time implementing this workshop, so any feedback
    is welcome! 🫶
:::
